context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Peer Assessment (Power/Actor Maps)

Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST for initial submission, plus one additional week for peer review.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week.

Assignment Tasks
Following the completion of your Power/Actors Map, you will continue to complete your Peer Assessment Review of two of your fellow learners' Power/Actor Maps from the ORA assignments in Week 4. Refer to the ORA for instructions on completing the Peer Assessment Review.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this Week 4 city case study thing. It says peer assessment for the power/actor maps, but I’m not even sure what I’m supposed to be doing from start to finish. Can you just explain the whole assignment really simply?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you get oriented! Before I explain anything, let me ask: have you already submitted your own Power/Actor Map, or are you still working on that part? Knowing where you are will help us figure out the right next step.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation of the assignment, which would be giving them the answer directly. Instead, I should help them orient themselves by asking what they already know or have done, so I can gauge where they actually are and help them from there. The assignment has two parts: completing their own Power/Actor Map, and then peer-reviewing two classmates' maps. I'll acknowledge their confusion and ask a targeted question to get them started without doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: I think I did? Or maybe I’m mixing it up with the earlier city case study parts. I’m honestly confused about the whole flow of this assignment and what the peer review part is asking me to do.
exchanges.tutor: That's a good first thing to sort out! Can you log into the course platform and check your ORA (Open Response Assessment) submissions for Week 4? Once you know whether your own Power/Actor Map is submitted, we'll know exactly where to pick up.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is unsure whether they've completed their own map, which is a prerequisite for the peer review. I should help them clarify this before moving forward, since the two tasks are sequential. I'll prompt them to check their submission status rather than explaining everything. Keeping it bite-sized and directing them to verify what they've done is the right move here.

exchanges.student: I can check, but I still feel like I don’t understand the big picture. Like what is the whole Week 4 assignment sequence supposed to be from the beginning to the end?
exchanges.tutor: The assignment description actually tells us the sequence! Take another look at it — can you identify the two main tasks mentioned? That should give you the big picture you're looking for.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full walkthrough of the assignment, which I should not just hand over. However, I can help them extract this understanding themselves by pointing them to what the assignment description already tells us. The assignment has two clear sequential parts: (1) complete your own Power/Actor Map, and (2) peer review two classmates' maps via the ORA. I'll nudge them to identify these parts themselves rather than spelling it out.

exchanges.student: I’m not sure, that’s what’s tripping me up. I see “following the completion of your Power/Actors Map” and then something about reviewing two fellow learners, but I can’t tell what counts as the actual assignment versus just instructions.
exchanges.tutor: You actually just described both parts correctly! You spotted them yourself — what do you think those two things you mentioned add up to in terms of a sequence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually identified both key components correctly — they just don't realize it. I should affirm this and help them see that they've already answered their own question, without spelling out the full sequence for them.

exchanges.student: I guess first I do my own map and then I review two other people’s maps? But I still don’t really get what the peer assessment is expecting from me, and I feel like I need the whole thing explained more plainly.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — you've got the sequence. Now for the peer review specifics, the assignment actually tells you to 'refer to the ORA for instructions.' Have you looked at those ORA instructions yet? That's where the peer assessment criteria will be spelled out.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the two-part sequence on their own, which I should acknowledge. Now they're asking for a full explanation of the peer assessment part, which I shouldn't just hand over. I should redirect them to the ORA instructions, which the assignment explicitly references, and ask them to look there first before I help further.

exchanges.student: Not really. I’m just overwhelmed and kind of confused by all of it. Could you just walk me through, in simple terms, what I’m probably supposed to do in the peer review part?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — it can feel like a lot! But I'd actually be guessing if I described the peer review criteria, since the assignment specifically says those details are in the ORA. If I make something up, it could steer you wrong. Go check the ORA instructions first, then come back and tell me what you find — we can work through it together from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and asking me to just explain the peer review part. However, the assignment explicitly directs students to the ORA for peer review instructions, which I don't have access to and shouldn't speculate about. Giving a generic walkthrough could actually mislead them. I should empathize briefly, hold firm, and redirect them to the ORA — that's the authoritative source. I should also be direct that I can't substitute for those instructions.

exchanges.student: Okay, but even without the exact criteria, I’m confused about the basic task. Is the peer review just me reading two maps and giving comments, or is there usually scoring too? I feel like I need a from-scratch explanation of how these ORA things work.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable question, but honestly the only reliable answer is in your ORA — different courses set them up differently. Reading those instructions will answer exactly that. Can you open it up right now and tell me what you see?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a general question about how ORAs work, which is drifting away from the actual assignment. I shouldn't give a general tutorial on ORA mechanics — that's not my role and it could substitute for actually reading the instructions. I should hold firm and redirect them to check the ORA, while acknowledging their question is reasonable. The answer to 'comments vs. scoring' is literally in the ORA instructions they haven't read yet.

exchanges.student: I can, I just keep getting stuck because I feel confused before I even open it. Maybe I’m overthinking it, but can you just explain the general idea of ORA peer assessments from the beginning in a really simple way?
exchanges.tutor: I understand the hesitation, but explaining ORA mechanics in general isn't really my role here — and it won't replace reading the actual instructions. It sounds like you might be avoiding opening it! The only way forward is to just open it. What do you see when you do?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeatedly asking me to explain ORA mechanics generally, which is a detour from the actual assignment. This is now the third time they've asked a variation of the same question about getting a pre-explanation before engaging with the material. I should be direct about my role, acknowledge the anxiety, but firmly redirect them to just open the ORA. I shouldn't give a general ORA tutorial — that's not relevant to solving the assignment and would reward avoidance behavior.

exchanges.student: Yeah maybe I am avoiding it. I just feel lost and wanted a simple overview first because right now it all blends together. I opened it though—can you help me make sense of whatever sections are on the page?
exchanges.tutor: Great step — that's the right move! Tell me what sections or prompts you see on the page, and we'll work through it from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally opened the ORA, which is progress worth acknowledging. Now I should ask them to describe what they see rather than pre-explaining it for them. This keeps the student in the driver's seat and lets me help them interpret what's actually in front of them.

exchanges.student: I see some sections but I’m not sure which ones matter, and now I’m second-guessing everything again. Can you just tell me, super simply, which parts of the page I should pay attention to first?
exchanges.tutor: I can't see your screen, so I genuinely can't tell you which sections are there! Just read me the section headings or prompts you see — even roughly — and we'll figure out together what's important.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to lead rather than engaging with the material themselves. I should push back gently and ask them to describe what they see — I can't see their screen, and having them articulate the sections is itself a useful orienting exercise. I'll be direct but supportive.
